text: Skills can be unlocked upon reaching certain character levels, after investing a certain number of passive points into a class, or after selecting a Mastery Class.

Opening the Skills Window (bound to the S key by default) allows you to see which skills your character can unlock, and how to do so. You can also click on any skill to view its <link="skill specialization">Specialization Tree</link>.
Clicking one of the 5 skill slots on your Action Bar at the bottom of the screen allows you to change which skill is equipped there.
Skill Specialization Slots are unlocked as your character levels up, to a maximum of 5 (to match how many skills can be equipped on the Action Bar).

Upon unlocking a Skill Specialization Slot, you can choose to Specialize in a skill that your character currently knows. Doing so allows you to level up that skill and invest points into its Specialization Tree. Skills have a natural maximum level of 20.
If you would like to specialize in a different skill, you can press the Respec button at the top right of the Skills Window while viewing the tree for a specialized skill.
Newly specialized skills will be automatically set to your <#E5E5E5>Minimum Skill Level</color>, and may receive a bonus to XP so they can catch up to your other skills.
You can see your current Minimum Skill Level on the Respec button inside a skill's tree. Skills currently affected by a XP bonus will state so above their XP bar on the left side of their tree.
Items can have affixes which grant additional levels to certain skills. These affixes can exceed the normal 20 level limit of skills. These are most commonly found in Helmets, Body Armors and Relics.
Any skill levels currently granted by items will be shown in blue above a skill's XP bar inside its skill tree.

Unequipping an item which grants levels to your skills will remove points from them, unless you have a number of unspent points greater than or equal to the levels lost.

Nodes which have lost points in this way will be highlighted in red when you next view that skill's tree.
All equipment has stats that are inherent to it, which are called implicits. For example, all Worn Boots have an increased movement speed implicit.
Each item subtype of an item always has the same implicits, but the values of implicits are randomized within a range.

All modifiers from implicits apply globally. For example, the “Increased Physical Damage” from a weapon’s implicit has the same effect as “Increased Physical Damage” from an <link="affixes">affix</link> on a ring or from a passive node.

Affixes are the modifiers on equipment that exist in addition to <link="implicits">Implicits</link>. They can be present on the item when it is dropped, or they can be added and modified later through <link="crafting explanation">Crafting</link> using <link="shards">Shards</link>. The number of affixes on an item often determines its <link="rarity">Rarity</link>.

Affixes have 7 tiers, with tier 1 being the lowest and tier 7 being the highest. However, only tier 1 through 5 can be created through <link="crafting">crafting</link>. Tier 6 and 7 affixes must be on the item when it drops.

The values of an affix at each tier fall within a range, but the lowest roll of a tier is always higher than the tier below it.

The value an affix has on an item can be re-rolled within its tier using a Rune of Refinement.
Affixes are broken into two main categories: prefixes and suffixes. An item can have up to 2 prefixes and 2 suffixes.

All stats granted by affixes are global. This means the “Increased Physical Damage” affix on a ring will have the same effect as “Increased Physical Damage” on a weapon’s <link="implicit">implicit</link> or a passive node.
There are 6 different rarities of equipment items in Last Epoch. Rarity is determined by what stats an item has, and can be changed by <link="crafting explanation">crafting</link>.
<#E5E5E5>Common (white):</color> The item does not have any <link="affixes">affixes</link>.
<#36A3E2>Magic (blue):</color> The item has either 1 or 2 affixes, which can be prefixes, suffixes, or both.
<#E3D157>Rare (yellow):</color> The item has either 3 or 4 affixes, up to 2 prefixes and 2 suffixes.
<#EB730A>Unique (orange):</color> The item has stats and effects that cannot be found on lower rarity items. Uniques cannot be crafted on.
<#71E87D>Set (green):</color> Set items are uniques that also grant bonuses for having other parts of the same set equipped. Sets will typically have 2 to 4 pieces.

<#C184FF>Exalted (purple): Exalted items have one or more tier 6 or tier 7 affixes.

Tier 6 and 7 affixes cannot be created through crafting, but crafting otherwise works the same for Exalted items as it does for Magic and Rare items.
<#E80B58>Legendary (red):</color> Legendary items are created in the <link="eternity cache">Eternity Cache</link> and are a combination of a Unique item and an Exalted item.

Increased Item Rarity is an effect that increases the average number of <link="affixes">affixes</link> on items, resulting in more rare and exalted items. It also increases the chance of finding unique and set items.
Increased Item Rarity modifier is most commonly seen in the endgame system <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>.

Uniques are a rarity of equipment items in Last Epoch.

Unique items can grant stats and special effects which do not appear as normal affixes on magic, rare or exalted items.
Uniques provide new synergies between existing mechanics, introduce entirely new mechanics, and more.

Uniques cannot be modified through <link="crafting">crafting</link>. They can however have an extra property called Legendary Potential which can be used in the creation of <link="Legendary Items">Legendary Items</link>.

Set items are very similar to uniques in terms of their design and what effects they offer. However, equipping multiple items from the same set grants additional bonuses, which are shown on their tooltips.

Sets will typically have between 2 and 4 pieces.

Set items are not able to be modified through <link="crafting">crafting</link>, however they can be shattered to Set Affixes in a <link="woven echoes">Woven Echo</link>. The Set Affixes created are dependant on the Set item which was shattered.

A Legendary item is a composite item made up of a unique and exalted item. Unique items must have atleast 1 Legendary Potential (up to 4), and the exalted item must have exactly 4 unsealed affixes. The exalted item can have a sealed affix, it just cannot be added to the Legendary item.

When combined in the Eternity Cache, you are granted a Legendary item. The Eternity Cache is located within the Temporal Sanctum <link="dungeon">dungeon</link>. Running higher tiers of the dungeon will allow you to create Legendaries out of higher level uniques.

The Ledendary item is the same as the unique item with affixes added to it from the exalted item. The number of affixes selected is equal to the legendary potential of the unique item used. When using the Eternity Cache at the end of Tier 2 or higher Temporal Sanctum, you can choose one affix to guarantee being moved from the exalted item. If the Unique item had more than 1 Legendary Potential, the rest of the affixes are chosen at random.

The quantity of legendary potential that a unique item drops with is influenced mainly by the level of the zone which it drops in, but corruption also increases it slightly. Increased rarity mods, character level or any other modifiers have no effect.

The amount of Legendary Potential that a unique item will generally drop with is relative to the item’s base power. High powered or very well scaling unique items are difficult to find with even one legendary potential, and low powered or leveling items are much more likely to have even more than one legendary potential. This is done so that most unique items have more similar overall power potential.
Idols are a type of equipment that have many powerful and niche effects.
Idols can be equipped in a special area on the right side of the inventory. You gain more space for equipping Idols by completing quests, up to 20 slots.
Idols come in sizes ranging from 1 x 1 all the way to 2 x 2, 1 x 4, and 4 x 1. Larger sizes grant more of each stat, and many stats are restricted to specific sizes.
Most Idols can only be equipped by a specific base class, and class specific Idols have their own sets of possible affixes.
Idols always drop with 1 prefix and 1 suffix and cannot be altered through <link="crafting">crafting</link>.
All crafting is done in the Crafting Window (Forge), which is opened with the F key by default. Crafting using Forge can be done anywhere at any time.

After placing an item in the slot at the top of the window, you can either add a <link="shard">Shard</link> to the item by selecting one of the affix slots to fill and choosing an available affix or choose a <link="runes and glyphs">Rune</link> by clicking on the Rune icon. You can also choose to use a <link="runes and glyphs">Glyph</link> in the Support Item slot, if applicable.

If an item has any <link="forging potential">Forging Potential</link> remaining, you can use Shards and Runes on it. When an item runs out of Forging Potential, you can't alter it further in the Forging Panel.
Shards can be used to add new affixes to items or to increase the tier of an existing affix by one. An item can have two prefixes and two suffixes, and each affix can be crafted up to tier 5.

When adding a shard to an item you have a chance to achieve a <#E5E5E5>Critical Success</color>, which upgrades the tier of a random affix on the item in addition to the normal upgrade shard provides. A Critical Success does not cost any crafting materials or Forging Potential, and cannot upgrade an affix above tier 5.

Shards drop from enemies and chests like other items, but can also be created by using a Rune of Shattering or Rune of Removal on an item.

Some shards (and the affixes they represent) only drop in higher level zones, while others are simply less common.

Shards that drop as items are placed in your Inventory when picked up, and can be moved to the Crafting Window (Forge) by pressing the Transfer Materials button. Shards gained from Shattering are automatically stored in the Material Storage.
Runes are modifier items that can have various effects on the item they are applied to.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Shattering:</color> Destroys an item, creating Shards from its affixes. The number of Shards created is random for each affix, equal to the tier of the affix at maximum and 1 at minimum.
<#E5E5E5>Rune of Refinement:</color> Rerolls the values for each affix but does not change their tiers. Each affix is rolled individually, so some may have better values than others.
<#E5E5E5>Rune of Removal:</color> Removes a random affix on an item, returning a number of shards equal to its tier.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Discovery: Adds random tier 1 affixes to all empty affix slots on an item. Has an increased chance of rolling rarer affixes. Does not cost Forging Potential, but cannot be applied to items with zero Forging Potential.
<#E5E5E5>Rune of Shaping:</color> Rerolls all of the implicits on an item.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Ascendance:</color> Changes the item into a unique or set item of the same item type. Using this on a Silver Ring could turn it into any unique ring, including ones that are not Silver Rings. However, the Silver Ring cannot be turned into a Helmet. None of the other properties of the base item are retained. Rune of Ascendance cannot create unique or set items that are exclusive drops. There are many unique and set items that are only obtainable from bosses, for example.
<#E5E5E5>Rune of Creation:</color> Duplicates the item, but reduces the Forging Potential of both the original and the copy to 0.
Glyphs are support items that can be used alongside Runes or Shards to affect the outcome of crafting.
<#E5E5E5>Glyph of Hope:</color> Modifies the outcome of a craft, granting it a 25% chance to have no Forging Potential cost.

<#E5E5E5>Glyph of Chaos: Modifies the outcome of a craft when upgrading an affix. Randomly changes the upgraded affix to a different one that can spawn on that item type. It cannot change a prefix into a suffix or vice versa.

For example, if you use a fire resistance shard to a raise the tier of a fire resistance suffix the tier would increase as normal and then it would change into a different affix such as cold resistance or increased health.
<#E5E5E5>Glyph of Order:</color> Modifies the outcome of a craft when upgrading an affix. Prevents the roll of an affix within its range changing when it is upgraded.

<#E5E5E5>Glyph of Despair: Has a chance to seal an affix instead of upgrading it. The sealed affix is moved to its own slot, leaving its old slot open for you to add a new affix. The sealed affix cannot be modified further and an item can only have one sealed affix. The chance to seal is higher when crafting on affixes of lower tiers, and is also higher for exalted items and items with more affixes.
<#E5E5E5>Glyph of Envy:</color> Changes the item’s subtype to a different one with the same class requirement when adding or upgrading an affix.

Forging Potential is a property that all normal, magic, rare and exalted equipment items have, which is used at the forge to perform crafting actions. Once an item runs out of Forging Potential, it can no longer be crafted on but is still able to be used. Higher rarity items will generally drop with more Forging Potential.

Each time you use a shard or rune in the forge, the Forging Potential of the item affected is reduced by a random amount inside a range. The amount of Forging Potential used can be modified through the use of <link="runes and glyphs">Glyphs</link>. As long as you have any amount of Forging Potential remaining, you are able to craft on the item in the forge.
Damage in Last Epoch can be dealt in two different ways: as a <#E5E5E5>hit</color>, or as <#E5E5E5>damage over time</color>.

A <#E5E5E5>damage over time effect deals damage to a target or area repeatedly over a duration.

The most common sources of damage over time are ailments such as ignite, bleed, and poison, which affect individual targets.

There are also damage over time effects that damage all targets within an area, such as Fire Aura, Tornado, Consecrated Ground, and many more.
Any source of damage that is <#E5E5E5>not damage over time is considered a <#E5E5E5>hit. Most damaging skills include a hit, such as Swipe, Rive, Fireball, Elemental Nova, and Rip Blood.

Hits can be <link="dodged">dodged</link>, <link="blocked">blocked</link>, or turned into a <link="glancing blow">glancing blow</link>, and there are many other effects that trigger either when you hit something or when you are hit by an enemy.

Damage over time cannot be mitigated by most defense mechanics, except for <link="resistances">resistances</link>. Damage over time cannot <link="critical strikes">critically strike</link> and does not trigger on hit effects such as Health Gained On Hit.
All skills have a base amount of damage that they would deal without any stats, which is called base damage. Base damage can only be modified by a skill’s tree.
The default base damage for each skill is listed on its tooltip.

Melee skills typically have 2 base damage total because most of their damage comes from weapons.

Spells and throwing attacks can have much more base damage, but the exact amount varies a lot between skills.

When calculating damage dealt, stats like “<link="+5">+5</link> melee fire damage” are multiplied by the skill’s <link="added damage effectiveness">added damage effectiveness</link>, then added to the base damage before stats like “30% <link="increased added more">increased</link> damage” and “10% <link="more">more</link> damage” are applied.

Added damage without a damage type, such +5 Melee Damage, adds damage of a type that depends on the base damage type of the skill. For example +5 Melee Damage will add 5 Melee Physical Damage to a Physical Melee Attack but 5 Melee Fire Damage to a Fire Melee Attack.

The base damage type of specific skills can be converted. For example, a Physical skill could be converted to a Fire skill. Conversions are typically found in skill trees and items, and sometimes in passive trees.

Base damage conversion converts a skill’s <link="base damage">base damage</link> from one type to another, which does not affect damage from stats such as “+5 Melee Fire Damage”, but will change the damage type of added damage stats without a damage type tag such as "+5 Melee Damage".
Damage conversion doesn't affect ailments such as ignite, or any other effects applied or triggered by the skill, unless stated otherwise.

All skills have an added damage effectiveness, which is a multiplier that is applied to all damage from stats like “+5 melee fire damage”.

Skills with large mana costs or cooldowns typically have greater than 100% damage effectiveness, while some skills that hit rapidly have less than 100%.

For example, Elemental Nova has a damage effectiveness of 100%, while Meteor has a damage effectiveness of 900%.

If you had “+10 spell fire damage” from your passives, 10 fire damage would be added to Elemental Nova, while 90 fire damage would be added to Meteor. You can view the damage effectiveness of skills by holding ALT while hovering on them.
Damage effectiveness does not affect <link="base damage">base damage</link>.
Resistances apply whenever you take damage, reducing how much damage you take. Resistances are one of the few ways to mitigate <link="damage over time">Damage over Time</link> effects.

If you have 10% physical resistance, you will take 10% less physical damage.

Resistances cap at 75%, so having a resistance value of greater than 75% will not directly reduce the damage you take.

Resistances can be reduced to negative numbers, with each 1% of negative resistance increasing damage taken of that type by 1%.

Having greater than 75% uncapped resistance protects against effects that reduce resistances such as Resistance Shreds, Shock or Marked for Death curse.

A stack of Shock reduces your lightning resistance by 2%, so if you had 75% lightning resistance it would be reduced to 73%.

If you had 77% lightning resistance, your final lightning resistance would still be 75%.
<link="penetration">Penetration</link> applies after your resistances are capped to 75%.
Armor mitigates the damage you take from all <link="hits">hits</link>, but has no effect against <link="damage over time">Damage over Time</link>.

The percentage of damage mitigated by armor depends on how much armor you have and the level of the area you are in.

The same amount of armor provides less mitigation at higher levels, and the percentage of damage mitigated cannot exceed 85%.
This is according to the following formula, where <#E5E5E5>x</color> is the amount of armor you have and <#E5E5E5>a</color> is the area level.
Armor is 70% as effective against non-physical damage.
Penetration subtracts from resistances, causing a source of damage to deal more damage.

Penetration applies after resistances are capped to 75%, and can reduce resistances to negative values.

For example, 30% lightning penetration against 20% lightning resistance will result in -10% lightning resistance.

All enemies gain 1% of penetration per area level, up to a maximum of 75%.

Players with less than 75% of each resistance at area level 75 and beyond will take increased damage due to having negative resistance values.
Ward is a shield above your health that is generated by certain effects and rapidly decays over time.
Ward has no maximum value, but will always decay towards 0 unless you continue to generate more or have a stat called Ward Decay Threshold.
Ward does not decay below your Ward Decay Threshold. For example, having 500 Ward Decay Threshold means that decay will not lower your Ward below 500, and Ward Decay rate at 1000 Ward is halved.
Dodge is a defensive mechanic that grants you a chance to completely avoid taking hits.
Your Dodge Chance is calculated from Dodge Rating and is lower in higher level areas. Dodge chance cannot go above 85%.
Where “a” is the zone’s area level and “x” is Dodge Rating.

A good rule of thumb is that having dodge rating equal to 10 times the area level will give you about 50% Dodge Chance.

A zone's area level can be viewed on the overlay map (tab by default).
Dodge cannot protect you from <link="damage over time">Damage over Time</link> effects, but dodging does not count as being <link="hit">hit</link>.
Block is a defensive mechanic that grants a chance to reduce the damage taken from hits.
Whenever you take a hit, your Block Chance is rolled to determine if you block the hit. On a successful block, a percentage of the damage is mitigated.
This percentage depends on how much Block Effectiveness you have, and the level of the area you are in. The percentage cannot exceed 85%.
Where “a” is the zone’s area level and “x” is Block Effectiveness.
You can block all hit (including from spells), but not <link="damage over time">damage over time</link> effects. Blocking a hit counts as being hit.
Endurance is a defensive mechanic that allows you to take less damage while below a certain health value.
While your health is less than your Endurance Threshold, you will take less damage equal to your Endurance value. This reduction applies to health, but does not apply to ward.
Endurance has a cap of 60% less damage taken (multiplicative with other modifiers), while Endurance Threshold does not have a cap.

Endurance applies to any damage taken below your Endurance Threshold, even if the instance of damage applied while you were above it. For example, if you take a hit of 100 damage, but 50 of that damage was below your Endurance Threshold, the last 50 damage will have Endurance applied to it.
All characters start with 20% Endurance and have an Endurance Threshold equal to 20% of their maximum health.
Endurance mitigates both <link="hits">hits</link> and <link="damage over time">damage over time</link>.

The "chance to receive a Glancing Blow" stat grants you a chance to take a Glancing Blow when hit.

A Glancing Blow reduces the damage you take from a hit by 35%. Having a chance greater than 100% has no additional effect.
Glancing Blow cannot protect you from <link="damage over time">Damage over Time</link> effects and taking a Glancing Blow counts as being <link="hit">hit</link>.
All hits have a chance to critically strike, or “crit”, dealing much more damage than normal. Damage over time effects cannot crit.
All hits have a base critical strike chance of 5%. This can be improved by items, passives, and skill tree nodes.

The difference between <#E5E5E5>added critical strike chance and <#E5E5E5>increased critical strike chance is very important.

<#E5E5E5>+1% critical strike chance will result in a final chance of 6% (assuming no other modifiers), while <#E5E5E5>1% increased critical strike chance will result in a final chance of 5.05%. Both stats together will result in a final chance of 6.06%.
When a hit crits, the damage dealt is multiplied by a critical strike multiplier, which is multiplicative with other modifiers. The default critical strike multiplier is 200%.

The <#E5E5E5>critical strike avoidance stat grants a chance to cause critical strikes to become normal hits instead. This chance is rolled after the attacker's crit chance.

If an enemy has 30% critical strike chance and you have 50% critical strike avoidance, the enemy will effectively have a critical strike chance of 15%.

All enemy hits that can critically strike have a critical strike multiplier of 200%, but this can be reduced by <#E5E5E5>less bonus damage taken from critical strikes<#E5E5E5>. You cannot have more than 100% less bonus damage taken from critical strikes, which reduces the damage of a critical strike to that of a regular hit.

Stats and modifiers in Last Epoch can exist in 3 main categories: <#E5E5E5>added, <#E5E5E5>increased, and <#E5E5E5>more. The category a modifier belongs to determines which step it will be used at in calculations. Not all stats will have modifiers for each category in practice.

<#E5E5E5>(5 + 2 + 3) = 10


First, all <#E5E5E5>added sources of the stat are totaled together.

<#E5E5E5>(1 + 0.05 + 0.3 + 0.1) = 1.45


Second, all <#E5E5E5>increased sources of the stat are totaled together. Increased values are percentages, so they are converted to decimal numbers and an additional 1 is added. <#E5E5E5>Reduced sources are subtracted from this same total.

<#E5E5E5>(10 x 1.45) = 14.5


Third, the total of the <#E5E5E5>added sources is multiplied by the total of the <#E5E5E5>increased sources.

<#E5E5E5>14.5 x (1 + 0.05) x (1 + 0.3) x (1 + 0.1) = 21.77


Finally, the product of the <#E5E5E5>added and <#E5E5E5>increased sources is multiplied by each <#E5E5E5>more source. <#E5E5E5>More values are also percentages, so 1 is added each time. <#E5E5E5>Less sources are also applied here as values subtracted from 1.

Note that the <#E5E5E5>more</color> sources are not added together first, which would result in a different number. If the <#E5E5E5>more</color> values in this example were totaled first, the final result would be 21.03, not 21.77.
Health Leech is a mechanic that allows you to recover a percentage of the damage you deal as health.

Each time you deal damage (whether from a hit or damage over time), any applicable leech stats are calculated, then the amount of damage is multiplied by this value to get the amount of health you will gain.

For example, if you deal 100 physical damage with a melee attack and you have “5% of melee damage leeched as health” and “3% of physical damage leeched as health”, you will leech <#E5E5E5>100 x (0.05 + 0.03) = 8 health.

The damage value used to calculate the health returned is the final damage dealt to the target, meaning defensive mechanics like resistances and block will reduce it.

If a target takes more damage than it has health remaining, the excess will not be used to calculate leech. For example, if an enemy has 25 health and you deal 100 damage to it, only 25 damage will be used for calculating leech.

Health returned by health leech is granted evenly over 3 seconds. Health from each instance of leech is granted independently, meaning they expire at different times. There is no limit to how much you can leech at once.
Stunned enemies and players cannot move or use skills. Stun has a base duration of 0.4 seconds.

All hits have a chance to stun if they deal enough damage. Hits that deal more damage are more likely to stun, but enemies that have higher maximum health are harder to stun.

The Increased Stun Chance stat also makes hits more likely to stun, while the target’s Stun Avoidance stat makes it harder to stun. If the target is shocked, it has an increased chance to be stunned.
The stun chance of any hit can be calculated using this formula:
When calculating stun chance using the formula above, melee damage dealt by players is multiplied by 3, and other damage dealt by players is multiplied by 2.

Due to the formula above, at 0% increased stun chance and 0 stun avoidance, non-player hits must deal more damage than 5% of a target's maximum health to have a chance to stun them. Every 100 stun avoidance raises this threshold by 1%. This threshold is reduced by increased stun chance, and by the player bonuses described above.
Players have inherent stun avoidance equal to 250 + 5 per character level, and can acquire more through items, passives and blessings.
Enemies and players that are frozen cannot move or use skills. Freeze has a base duration of 1.2 seconds.
To have a chance to freeze, a skill must have a <#E5E5E5>freeze rate</color>. Most Cold skills have a freeze rate, which is listed on their tooltips.

Your skills' base freeze rate listed in their tooltips can be increased by the "freeze rate per stack of chill" stat, and that final number is then multiplied by the "freeze rate multiplier" stat (which is 100% by default). If the target is <link="frostbite">frostbitten</link>, it has an increased chance to be frozen. It is more difficult to freeze enemies that have more maximum health and current ward.
Minions are allies of the player that are summoned by skills, items, passives, or other effects.

Minions automatically attack enemies and will run towards the player if they’ve strayed too far behind.

You can also manually tell your minions to target the enemy under your cursor using Minion Attack Command (bound to A by default). If there’s no enemy at the location you target, your minions will move there before picking a new target to attack. You can also use this command to have your minions break things like crates. Minions have two modes, Protect and Assassinate, which alter the distance they attack enemies or run back to the player. In Assassinate mode minions prioritize attacking targets close to them, especially bosses and rare enemies, and don't return to the player unless they're very far away. In Protect mode minions stay closer to the player and prioritize targets close to the player instead.

Minions summoned by a skill will typically gain additional stats from skill tree nodes. Modifiers your character has (such as from passives and items) <#E5E5E5>will not apply unless minions are specified.

For example, a passive node that grants <#E5E5E5>increased physical damage will not apply to your minions, but <#E5E5E5>increased minion physical damage will apply.
A minion will prioritize using a skill that has just come off of cooldown if it has multiple to use, assuming there is a valid target in range.
<link="companions">Companions</link> and <link="totems">Totems</link> are two types of minions that have additional mechanics.

Companions are a type of minion with special mechanics used primarily the Primalist class. By default, you can have 2 companions active at once.

All minion stats and effects apply to companions, but there are effects that only benefit companions.

After using a skill to summon a companion, the summon skill will be replaced by a companion ability, which is specific to the companion summoned.

If you can summon multiple of the same companion, you must summon the maximum number or reach your companion limit before the ability will change.

When a companion’s health is reduced to zero, it enters a <#E5E5E5>Downed</color> state rather than dying. Being close to a downed companion will revive it, but if you wait too long the companion will die and must be summoned again.
Totems are a type of <link="minions">minion</link> that is used by the Primalist class.
Totems cannot move and have a fixed duration in addition to health. All minion stats and effects apply to totems, but there are effects that only benefit totems.

Potions are a special type of item that drops from killing enemies or hitting bosses. Potions can be used on demand for a quick burst of healing. You can pick up a potion by walking near it while you have empty potion slots or you are not at full health. When you pick up a potion while you are at max potions but not full health, the potion will be automatically consumed and you are healed. You can increase your maximum potion carrying capacity by upgrading your belt.
The Monolith of Fate is the main endgame system in Last Epoch. Characters can access the Monolith of Fate from the End of Time, though it is designed to be started after the campaign.
The Monolith of Fate consists of multiple <link="timeline">Timelines</link>. All Timelines have a fixed area level and their own set of layouts, enemies and quests to encounter.

When you first start a Timeline there are multiple paths from the center island, with an <link="echoes">Echo</link> on each. After you complete an Echo, the Echoes behind are also revealed. You can do any Echo you've unlocked at any time.

Completing Echoes grants Timeline Stability. After you reach a certain threshold, you can select a <link="quest echo">Quest Echo</link> from the top left side of the screen. After completing all Quest Echoes in a Timeline, your run is completed and you can choose a <link="blessing">Blessing</link> that grants different bonuses, such as character power or specific item drop rate benefits.
A Timeline represents an alternate reality that never was, or never will be.
All <link="echoes">Echoes</link> you encounter in a Timeline have the same area level, and each Timeline has a unique pool of layouts, enemies and quests.

Completing all <link="quest echoes">Quest Echoes</link> for a Timeline unlocks the next timeline and grants you the opportunity to choose a <link="blessing">Blessing</link>. Additionally, conquering the most difficult Timelines will unlock the <link="empowered timelines">Empowered Timelines</link>.
Each zone you encounter in a <link="timeline">Timeline</link> is called an Echo.

An Echo has a layout based on its name, and a randomly chosen modifier that it will add to enemies. The enemy modifier also affects the rarity of items you will find and how much experience you will earn.

As you conquer more Echoes in a row, the modifiers you select will start to persist for multiple Echoes. This means you will have multiple modifiers active at the same time.

Conquering each Echo grants Timeline Stability, which is required for unlocking Quest Echo.
Echoes have a guaranteed reward for completing them. However, if you die before completing an Echo you won't be able to collect it.
As you progress further from the center of a Timeline, you will start to find echoes with the Shade of Orobyss within.

Defeating the Shade adds Corruption to the timeline, increasing its difficulty and rewards and certain items dropped by the Shade require a minimum amount of Corruption. It also brings the echo web for the timeline back to a fresh state, allowing you to start again with a higher potential for rewards.
Defeating the Shade does not reset your Timeline Stability or the active modifiers you have.
Corruption increases item rarity, experience gained, monster health, monster damage and how much Timeline Stability echoes give.

Corruption also increases area level in standard timelines, and increases the rarity of guaranteed echo rewards in Empowered timelines. Corruption also, very slightly, increases the odds of unique items having <link="legendary potential">Legendary Potential</link> or <link="weavers will">Weaver's Will</link>.

After conquering a certain amount of Timeline Stability in a <link="timeline">Timeline</link>, you will have the chance to encounter a Quest Echo, which represents a pivotal moment in the story of a Timeline.
The final Quest Echo in a Timeline is a boss fight, and by defeating it the adjacent Timelines will become unlocked.

Quest Echoes appear in the same order each time, and all Quest Echoes for the current Timeline need to be completed to unlock the next Timeline. In <link="empowered timelines">Empowered Timelines</link>, Quest Echoes are optional and are not required for challenging the boss of the Timeline.
Dying in a Quest Echo will reduce your Timeline Stability, but cannot undo your progression through the Quest Echoes.

Blessings are permanent increases to your character's stats or chance to find particular types of items.

Blessings apply throughout the entire game, not just in the Monolith of Fate.

When you conquer a <link="timeline">Timeline</link> you will be presented with 3 Blessings to choose from. If you conquer the same timeline again, you can choose to either keep your current Blessing, or replace it with a new one.

Each Timeline has its own set of possible Blessings. Some Timelines have Blessings that increase your character's stats, while others have Blessings that increase your chance to find particular types of items.

You can view every blessing you have discovered and currently have active from the blessings tab in the inventory window. You can also hover on a Timeline in the world map and see your current Blessing from that timeline.

Blessings granted by Empowered Timelines have higher values than the versions granted by normal Timelines. Completing an Empowered Timeline has 4 Blessing options by default, and 5th Blessing option is unlocked at 200 <link="corruption and the shade">Corruption</link>.
An ailment is an effect on a target that lasts for a duration. Most ailments deal damage, apply stat debuffs, or both, and most can be applied to the same target multiple times (or "stack").
Ailments can either be applied by a <link="hit">hit</link>, or by area effects that attempt to apply ailments at a fixed interval.

Most ailment applications are chance based. However, having an ailment chance greater than 100% allows you to apply multiple stacks at once if that ailment can stack.

For example, if you have 235% ignite chance with Fireball, each Fireball hit will apply 2 stacks of ignite, with a 35% chance to apply a 3rd stack of ignite.
Each damaging ailment, such as Bleed or Ignite, has a fixed base damage, but is affected by damage modifiers from the skill tree and attribute scaling of the skill that applies it.
Even when an ailment is applied by a melee skill or a hit, that does not make it count as melee damage or hit damage and does not cause it to be affected by stats like increased melee damage.
If a target has the maximum number of stacks for a given ailment, attempting to apply another stack of that ailment will replace the oldest stack.

The most common ailments are described in other pages within this section. If an ailment isn't described here, the tooltip (or alt tooltip) of the node or item that grants it should explain the ailment's functionality.

The duration of an ailment can be lengthened by "increased ailment duration" effects. 100% increased bleed duration will make a stack of bleed last 6 seconds, rather than the default 3 seconds. "Reduced ailment duration" effects are also possible.


Changing the duration of an ailment that deals damage affects the total amount of damage it will deal, but does not affect damage per second (DPS). This results in its increasing the total damage the ailment deals over its duration.


For example if a stack of Bleed dealt 120 damage over its default 3 second duration, then a 50% increased duration modifier would result in it dealing 180 damage over 4.5 seconds.

Increased ailment effectiveness effects apply to all stat modifiers an ailment grants.


For example, 50% increased armor shred effectiveness would cause stacks of armor shred to reduce armor by 150, instead of the default 100.

Each damage type (Physical, Cold, Fire, Lightning, Necrotic, Poison and Void) has a respective Resistance Shred ailment, such as Fire Resistance Shred.

Resistance Shred ailments reduce the target's resistance for that particular damage type. They subtract 5% resistance against most enemies, but only 2% against players and bosses. They last 4 seconds by default and stack up to 10 times.

Like penetration, resistance shred can bring effective resistance below 0%, further increasing damage taken. Most enemies have 0% resistance, so 10 stacks of Fire Resistance Shred will result in them having -50% Fire Resistance and consequently taking 50% more fire damage.
Armor Shred reduces <link="armor">armor</link> on the target, increasing how much damage it takes from hits. By default it subtracts 100 armor and lasts for 4 seconds.
Bleed deals physical damage over time. It can stack an unlimited number of times. By default each stack deals 53 damage over a 3 second duration.

Each stack of Chill reduces attack speed, cast speed and movement speed by 12%. This effect is multiplicative with other modifiers and each stack is multiplicative with the other stacks of Chill.

Chill can stack up to 3 times and lasts 4 seconds by default.

Chill has 50% less effect against bosses and players (multiplicative with other modifiers), so each stack reduces attack speed, cast speed and movement speed by 6% instead.

Damned deals necrotic damage over time and also reduces health regeneration. It can stack an unlimited number of times.

By default each stack deals 35 damage over a 2.5 second duration and applies a 20% reduced health regeneration modifier.
Frostbite deals cold damage over time and also increases the chance to be frozen. It can stack an unlimited number of times, but only the first 15 stacks on a target increase its chance to be frozen.
Ignite deals fire damage over time. It can stack an unlimited number of times. By default each stack deals 40 damage over a 2.5 second duration.

Poison deals poison damage over time and also reduces poison resistance. It can stack an unlimited number of times, but only the first 30 stacks on a target reduce poison resistance.

By default each stack deals 28 damage over a 3 second duration and subtracts 5% from the target's poison resistance (up to a maximum of 150% at 30 stacks).

Against bosses and players poison only subtracts 2% from poison resistance rather than 5% (up to a maximum of 60% at 30 stacks).

Shock reduces lightning resistance by 5% and increases the chance that the target will be stunned by 20%. Shock can stack up to 10 times and lasts 4 seconds by default.

Shock is 60% less effective against bosses and players, resulting in it only reducing lightning resistance by 2%, and only increasing chance to be stunned by 8%.

Each stack of Slow reduces movement speed by 20%. This effect is multiplicative with other modifiers and each stack is multiplicative with the other stacks of Slow.

Slow can stack up to 3 times and lasts 4 seconds by default.

Slow has 50% less effect against bosses and players (multiplicative with other modifiers), so each stack reduces movement speed by 10% instead.

Time Rot deals void damage over time, while also making stuns last longer. By default each stack deals 60 void damage over a 3 second duration and increases stun duration by 5%.

Time Rot can stack up to 12 times
Electrify deals lightning damage over time. It can stack an unlimited number of times. By default each stack deals 44 damage over a 2.5 second duration.

Frailty makes the affected target deal 6% less damage (multiplicative with other modifiers) and lasts for 4 seconds.

Frailty can stack 3 times.
High Health: Anything is considered to be at high health if it has at least 65% of its maximum health remaining.
Low Health: Anything is considered to be at low health if it has at most 35% of its maximum health remaining.
Recently: Recently refers to a duration of 4 seconds.

Dungeons are special challenges throughout the game with a special mechanic, a boss and unique rewards for each one. Entering a Dungeon requires a specific Key to that Dungeon, and there are 4 tiers for each Dungeon with increasing difficulty and rewards.

Keys for each Dungeon start dropping at a higher level than the Tier 1 level of the Dungeon, so you’re likely to come back to them later for their rewards, or use them on subsequent characters. Right clicking a Dungeon Key will show its location on the world map.
Body Armor, Helmets, Relics, Uniques and Sets can all have affixes which grant additional levels to certain skills. These affixes can exceed the normal 20 level limit of skills.
Any skill levels currently granted by items will be shown in blue above a skill's XP bar inside its skill tree.

Unequipping an item which grants levels to your skills will remove points from them, unless you have a number of unspent points greater than or equal to the levels lost.

Nodes which have lost points in this way will be highlighted in red when you next view that skill's tree.
You can change your preferred server region at the login screen, but also in-game in the Gameplay section of the Settings window.

If you are the leader of a party, you can change what region all servers for your party will be in by going to the Party tab of the Social menu (hotkey H by default). You can use this to pick a region that is between you and your party members to avoid large differences in latency, for example.

When you change your region setting in-game, the region of your current server won't change. The next time you request a new server it will be in your new preferred region. Monolith and Arena gameplay happens on a single server, so you will need to go to another type of zone first for the change to apply.
Item Factions provide you different ways to obtain items, and you get the option to align with one of them for the first time during Chapter 9 of the campaign.

These two Item Factions let you choose to either enable trading with other players, or to improve your ability to find more and better items. While you can only be aligned with one Item Faction at a time, it is not a permanent decision and you can choose to switch your alignment. More information can be found from Factions Window (bound to Y by default).

Aligning with the <link="merchant's guild">Merchant's Guild</link> enables trading with other players through the <link="merchant's guild">Bazaar</link> and aligning with the <link="circle of fortune">Circle of Fortune</link> enhances item drops and allows you to seek powerful Prophecies from the <link="circle of fortune">Observatory</link> to help find more and better items.

There are restrictions to equipping items depending which Factions’ services were used to obtain them. For example, many items found while aligned with Circle of Fortune will require the player to be aligned with Circle of Fortune to equip them. Similarly, when an item is traded within Merchant’s Guild, no player aligned with Circle of Fortune will be able to equip it.

While aligned with either Faction and slaying enemies or completing quests you gain a resource called <link="terminology">Favor</link>. You then expend Favor to use that Faction’s services and gain <link="terminology">Reputation</link>, which is your experience with a Faction. As your Reputation grows, you will gain <link="terminology">Ranks</link> within the Faction which unlocks better benefits and rewards within the Faction. Reputation is also gained from experience. The total Reputation gain is equal between the Factions if all Favor is spent, and roughly half of the Reputation comes from spending Favor while the other half comes from just gaining experience.

Merchant’s Guild is an <link="item factions">Item Faction</link> that enables trading with other players who are also aligned with Merchant’s Guild within the same <link="season">Season</link> and <link="game mode">Game Mode</link> (Normal or Hardcore). You’ll be able to align with them for the first time during Chapter 9 of the campaign. All trades require exchanging gold for items, no item-to-item trades are possible.
When aligned with the Merchant’s Guild for the first time, you’ll start at rank 1 which enables selling all equippable items that aren’t untradable, and the purchases of Normal, Magic and Rare items.

All trades done at the Bazaar require Favor, a currency gained while slaying enemies and completing quests while aligned with Merchant’s Guild. Selling items requires much less Favor than purchasing them.

Ranks can be increased by gaining Reputation with the Faction, which is done by gaining experience and spending Favor by buying and selling items, or spending it at the Merchant Guild’s representative. Higher ranks unlock more options to purchase valuable items, and maximum rank allows for any equippable item to be purchased. Open the Factions Window for more details (bound to Y by default).

The Bazaar is a town area near Maj’elka in Divine Era, visited in Chapter 9 during the campaign. Filled with stalls for different item types, players are able to search and filter precisely what they want to buy from other players. Trading through Bazaar is asynchronous, meaning you will not need to interact with other players to complete trades. Instead, buying an item will send it to your inventory if you have space for it.

Circle of Fortune is an Item Faction that grants powerful benefits towards finding more and better items. You’ll be able to align with the Circle of Fortune for the first time during Chapter 9. Unlike the <link="merchant's guild">Merchant's Guild</link>, items found while aligned with Circle of Fortune will always be untradable (they can still be <link="item gifting">gifted</link>) and if found from Prophecies or through Rank rewards, will also be bound to Circle of Fortune, making it impossible to equip them if aligned with the Merchant’s Guild.

When aligning with the Circle of Fortune for the first time, you’ll start at rank 1. As you gain Reputation from gaining experience or spending Favor with the Faction by either purchasing Prophecies at the Observatory or spending at the Faction’s representative, you’ll gain higher ranks which yields greater benefits from the Faction. For more details on the benefits from ranks gained while aligned with Circle of Fortune, you can open the Factions panel (bound to Y by default).

The Observatory is a town area where players aligned with Circle of Fortune are able to seek out Prophecies from Telescopes for Favor. Prophecies cause certain items to drop when completing a specific event, like slaying the boss of a specific <link="dungeons">Dungeon</link>.

Faction progress is shared stash-wide, which means that any character that has access to a stash with a character that has any Faction progress, will also share that progress. Once a <link="season">Season</link> ends, all Item Faction progress is moved to the <link="game mode">Legacy</link> environment, keeping the progress from the further progression.

After you have aligned with an Item Faction with one character, future characters sharing that stash are able to align with a Faction in the first town during Chapter 1, retaining the ranks you’ve gained with that Faction from previous character(s).

Items can only be traded once, and any item traded once will become untradable and bound to Merchant’s Guild, making it impossible to equip as a member of the <link="circle of fortune">Circle of Fortune</link> even if you switch Factions.

The Observatory also contains a Faction representative that can be used to spend Favor by purchasing equippable items and Lenses. Lenses are items that can be used to customize the Prophecies to further target what items are rewarded and types of events Prophecies require to complete. Higher ranks within the Faction allow deeper customization of the Telescope with Lenses.

In Last Epoch, characters have an attribute or attributes that represent what they excel at. For example, Rogue’s core attribute is Dexterity, while it’s Intelligence for the Mage. Each of these core attributes has a small benefit per point:
Strength grants 4% Increased Armor per point and improves the skills that rely on raw strength.
Dexterity grants 4 Dodge Rating per point and improves the skills that rely on skillful handling of weapons.
Intelligence grants 2% Ward Retention per point and improves the skills that rely on carefully studied magic.
Attunement grants 2 Mana per point and improves the skills that rely on the innate magic inside you and your surroundings.
Vitality grants 6 Health and 1% Poison and Nerotic Resistance per point.

As seen in the descriptions of the attributes above, they often improve skills with different expertises, and in most cases skills of a certain class by default benefit from the same stat. In some cases, more than one attribute can also scale a skill, like Primalist’s Gathering Storm that benefits from both Strength and Attunement. You can see what attribute scales a skill by hovering to the skill, and more detailed effect by holding ALT.
There are also ways to modify skills, or your character to gain additional benefits from attributes by selecting skill points, passive points or wearing items with those types of effects.

While slaying enemies and completing quests, you gain experience and after gaining a certain amount of experience, your character will Level Up. The maximum character level is 100, and different bonuses, skills and unlocks are gained at different levels.

For each level up, character gains the following stats:
- 10 Health (total +990 at level 100).
- 0.5 Mana (total +50 at level 100).
- 5 Stun Avoidance (total +495 at level 100).
- 0.125 Health Regeneration (total +12 at level 100).
- 5 Health Gained on Potion use (total +495 at level 100).
- Health gained for minions (hold ALT on a Minion Skill to see specifics).

Other leveling up benefits are:
- Passive Skill Point from 3 to 100 (total 98).
- Skill Specialization unlocks at levels 4, 8, 20, 35 and 50.
- Minimum Skill Levels increases at levels 8, 12, 17, 24, 32, 40, 50, 60 and 70.
- Certain Skills unlocked in your base Class.
- Ability to craft and equip items with a higher level requirement.
- 0.6 Minion Power from levels 26 to 100 (total 45% at level 100) which multiplicatively increases the damage your Minions deal, and lowers the damage they take.

Parry is a defensive mechanic that grants a chance to negate all damage taken from <link="hits">hits</link>. However unlike <link="dodge">Dodge</link>, Parrying does still counts as being hit and as a result of that ailments can still be applied to you when you parry a hit.
Parrying has no effect against <link="damage over time">Damage over Time</link> effects and is capped at 75%.

Equippable items found while playing together with other players in an area are able to be gifted to everyone who was present as the item was found with no time limit, and has no costs unlike <link="merchant's guild">trading</link>. Gifting can be done from the interact-menu for other players.

When you have received a gift, an icon in the bottom-right corner of the screen indicates it. Gifting Window can be opened by clicking that icon, or by from your Inventory (bound to I by default) and clicking the Gifting -button.

Resonances are special items that expand <link="item gifting">Item Gifting</link>, allowing you to gift specific items to players you play with frequently, even if the item didn’t drop while you were playing with them. Resonances are specific to an individual player. For example, a Golden Resonance that resonates with Moxjet200 (can be seen in the tooltip of the Resonance) allows you to gift an item to Moxjet200.

There are two different types of Resonance that allow you to gift different varieties of items. Both types of Resonances are rare, but Golden Resonances are the more common of the two. They allow you to gift any normal, magic, rare, set, or unique item. Obsidian Resonance are much rarer, and allow you to gift exalted and <link="legendary items">Legendary items</link>.

Slaying enemies that grant experience have a chance to grant progress towards a specific Resonance to drop, and once a certain amount of progress is gained, the Resonance will drop. This progress is cumulative between different play sessions and characters within a <link="game mode">Game Mode</link>, so when they share a stash, and only resets for a specific Resonance type when it is found.

Gifting an item with Resonance that has any restriction does not remove or change that restriction. For example, an item that requires to be aligned with <link="circle of fortune">Circle of Fortune</link> to be equipped will still have that requirement after it was gifted.

Resonances are stored when you press the “Transfer Materials” button in your inventory, and can be found in your Gifting window. You can open your gifting window by pressing “Gifting” button in your inventory.

Frequently Asked Questions:

Can I gain experience at max level (100)?

- Yes.

Can I gain experience at high level while playing in a low level area? For example, level 99 in a level 3 area.

- Yes. You won’t get much experience, but slaying any enemy that grants experience can generate progress, and the amount doesn’t matter.

Can I generate progress towards a Resonance just by being in a party with someone?
- No. You need to be in the same area and within a relatively close proximity. The distance is similar to experience gain range.

Can I track the progress towards a specific Resonance somehow?

- No, the progress towards Resonance drops is hidden.

When creating a new character, on top of deciding whether a character will be a <link="season">Season</link> or <link="legacy">Legacy</link> character, there’s also other options: Game Mode and Challenges.

There are two Game Modes in Last Epoch, Normal and Hardcore. The differences of the two are that they do not share stashes between eachother, and if a Hardcore character dies, it will become a Normal character of the same challenge and Season.
There’s also additional <link="challenges">Challenges</link> that can be chosen to further customize your experience.

When creating a character, on top choosing a <link="game mode">Game Mode</link>, additional challenges can be chosen: Solo Account Found (SAF) or Solo Character Found (SCF). This is not a permanent decision and you can opt-out from challenges in options.

Solo Account Mode (SAF) characters are unable to party with nor obtain items found by other players. This also includes any items found with Solo Character Found characters even if they share the same game mode and Season. However, items found by other Solo Account Mode characters will share the stash and <link="item factions">Item Faction</link> progress in the same game mode and Season

All characters created in Offline are by default Solo Account Found characters, unless opted-in for Solo Character Found. You can opt-out of Solo Account Found and turn the character to just Normal or Hardcore character with no extra challenges if it's an Online character.

Solo Character Found (SCF) is an added challenge to Solo where only items found by that character can be used by it, and no stash or Item Faction progress is shared with any other characters, even by your other Solo Character Found characters. You can opt-out of Solo Account Mode and turn the character to Solo Account Found to gain access to shared progress of other Solo Account Mode characters.

Legacy is the permanently existing environment in Last Epoch, where all your previous characters and progress from <link="season">Season</link> will be transferred over to when those Season end. Legacy does not have competitive leaderboards that can be participated in. While a Season is active, some features from that Season may not be included or obtainable in Legacy. An example of that could be Season-restricted items. Currently, there are no Season-restricted items in Last Epoch.

Seasons are periods of time with new content additions, changes to game balance and the reset of competitive leaderboards. It is also a fresh start where everyone making a character at the start of a new Season will start from scratch.

While Season is active, you can choose to create a character in the current Season, or in Legacy. All Seasonal characters will transfer to <link="legacy">Legacy</link> at the end of a Season and all progress will carry over and will not be deleted.
During a Season, some features may only be available in the Seasonal environment. An example of that could be Season-restricted items.
If dying with a character that is playing Hardcore <link="game mode">Game Mode</link> in a Season, that character will not become a Normal Legacy character, but instead Normal Season character.

Arena is one of the endgame activies in Last Epoch, and is located in Champion’s Gate. If you have an Arena Key or Arena Key of Memory, you can right click it and it’ll open the world map and show the location of Champion’s Gate.
There’s two different modes in Arena: Arena Champions and Endless Arena.

Arena Champions consists of 40 waves with additional rewards that can be chosen, and eventually culminates in fighting an Arena Champion. There are 4 different tiers of Arena Champions with better rewards at higher tiers.

Endless Arena is a mode with unlimited waves that progressively become more and more difficult, and the best Arena runs will be visible in the Leaderboards (opened by pressing L by default). Leaderboards are only available in Online play in a <link="season">Season</link>. Climb the waves in Endless Arena to show how good you and your builds are!
Characters below level 50 will not be able to benefit from experience bonuses in Endless Arena.

Loot Filter is a tool that helps in filtering out items you might not want to pick up, or to enhance the display of items you do want to pick up. Having a Loot Filter feature in the game helps in allowing the loot drops to be plentiful and customizable to your specific needs. You can open the Loot Filter User Interface by clicking SHIFT + F by default.
A single Loot Filter can have up to 75 individual rules, and these rules can be freely moved around or disabled without needing to delete them.

The rules at the top of the the filter take priority over rules below them. For example, if the top rule in the filter would be “show Unique helmets”, and a rule below it that would be “hide all helmets”, you’d see all Unique helmets while rest of them would be hidden.

Loot Filters can be exported and imported directly in the User Interface without closing the game. Filters exist in a folder that can be opened from the UI, and placing another filter there will make it available to be used in-game, but a filter can also be imported directly from copying it if your clipboard has a valid Loot Filter saved on it.
When a Loot Filter is enabled, it is possible to temporarily disable it by holding a keybind (X by default).
