Curses are special Ailments with their own scaling tag, do not usually stack with themselves, and have different effects. Some of them do damage which can be increased, while others might just be a debuff on the target, and some having both properties. Some curses are also specific skills with their own <link="skill specialization">specializable skill trees</link>, such as Bone Curse and Spirit Plague.

Spirit Plague is a specializable curse skill for Acolyte. It curses an enemy with an effect that deals 90 necrotic damage over time and spreads to another nearby enemy if it dies. Does not stack and lasts 3 seconds.

Bone Curse is a specializable curse skill for Acolyte. It curses enemies with a debuff that causes them to take physical damage when they are hit. Damage dealt by Bone Curse is trippled if you inflicted the hit. Does not stack and lasts 8 seconds.
Marked for Death is a curse that reduces all resistances by 25 and lasts 8 seconds. Does not stack.

Decrepify is a curse that causes enemies to take 15% more damage over time (multiplicative with other modifiers), deals physical damage over time based on your flat missing health and lasts 10 seconds. Does not stack.
Torment is a curse that deals 120 necrotic damage over time, reduces movement speed by 18% (multiplicative with other modifiers) and lasts 3 seconds. Does not stack.
Acid Skin is a curse that deals 50 poison damage over time, adds a +25% chance to be critically hit and lasts 5 seconds. Does not stack.

Penance is a curse which deals 20 fire damage to a target when hit and lasts 15 seconds. Penance also gains 5% more damage per 1% of Damage Reflected. Flat reflect does not increase the damage of Penance. Does not stack.

Anguish is a curse that reduces the damage over time dealt by enemies by 15% (multiplicative with other modifiers). When you kill any enemy, enemies cursed by Anguish take 40 necrotic damage. Does not stack.

Modifiers in the Monolith of Fate are special effects that make Echoes more difficult in various different ways and more rewarding by providing <link="increased item rarity">Increased Item Rarity</link> and experience gained, similar to Corruption. The difference with modifiers is that they last from 1 to 3 Echoes total instead of globally to all Echoes, have varies different effects and aren't usually simply just more monster damage and health. Stacking several modifiers will further increase the difficult and rewards.

More challenging modifiers provide higher benefits.

Experience bonuses from Monolith of Fate modifiers and corruption does not apply to characters below level 50.
Items can also be gifted with the use of <link="resonance">Resonance</link> in situations where a player was not present at the time of the item dropping.
Standard timelines start at 0 corruption and have a maximum of 50. Empowered timelines start at 100 corruption and have no maximum.

Empowered Timelines are harder versions of normal Timelines that start with 100 <link="corruption and the shade">Corruption</link>, and have no maximum Corruption. This means that Empowered Timelines can be scaled in difficulty until your character can no longer handle the difficulty and is unable to progress further.
Increasing Corruption increases the damage dealt and health of all enemies, and grants increased experience gained and increased item rarity.
Each Timeline has an Exclusive Echo Reward type that makes it easier to target farm those specific item types, and in Empowered Monoliths these are much more likely to spawn as rewards.

Weaver’s Will is a special mechanic on certain unique items representing the amount of total Affix Tiers the Unique item will gain at maximum when turned to a <link="legendary items">Legendary item</link>.
Instead of combining items in the <link="legendary items">Eternity Cache</link>, Weaver’s WIll uniques gain additional stats when slaying enemies and gaining experience while having it equipped.

When Weaver’s Will Unique gains a stat, it can either add a new tier 1 affix or upgrade an existing one, limited to tier 7. A Weaver’s WIll item with 28 Weaver’s WIll would eventually become a Legendary version of that unique with 4 tier 7 Affixes.
Weaver’s WIll Unique items found in higher level areas and higher <link="corruption and the shade">Corruption</link> will have an increased chance in having a higher Weaver’s Will.

Experimental Items are items that at first glance appear to just be Magic, Rare or Exalted items. However, they have a special <link="affixes">affix</link> that only appears on items found from encountering and defeating <link="exiled mages">Exiled Mages</link>, and cannot be <link="crafting explanation">crafted</link> by conventional means. Experimental affixes only appear on Boots, Belts and Gloves.
In the <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link> you might find a <link="woven echoes">Woven Echo</link>, which could unravel the mechanism for an unconventional crafting method.

Capped Resistance: The maximum resistance you can have. Resistance is capped at 75%. <link="penetrations">Penetration</link> applies against your capped resistance, so a hit that penetrates 10% of your Fire Resistance for example would deal the same damage to you if you had 75% Fire Resistance, or 200% Fire Resistance.

Uncapped Resistance: Uncapped resistance is the total amount of resistance you have against a specific damage type, ignoring the cap of 75%. Uncapped resistance can be useful in mitigating the effect of ailments that reduce your resistance, such as <link="shock">Shock</link>, <link="resistance shred">Resistance Shreds</link> or <link="curse">Marked for Death</link>, but does not help against Penetration past the cap of 75%.
Favor: Currency obtained from slaying enemies and completing quests that can be spent to gain Reputation with <link="item factions">Item Factions</link>.
Reputation: Your experience with an Item Faction. After gaining enough Reputation you’ll Rank up with your aligned Faction.

Rank: Your level with an Item Faction. Higher your Rank with an Item Faction, the better benefits and perks within the Faction. For example, when aligned with Merchant’s Guild higher rank allows you to purchase higher tier items.

Completing Dungeons also provide a skip through certain parts of the campaign, as the entrance point is earlier in the campaign while the exit from the Dungeon will be further ahead in a different point in the campaign. Completing a Dungeon for the first time can also grant two passive skill points and one idol slot upgrade.

As previously mentioned, each Dungeon has a special mechanic, and in order to complete the Dungeon it is strongly recommended to interact with it. This is done by using the skill that is provided inside each Dungeon (used with D by default).

Lightless Arbor is a Dungeon where you traverse through darkness with a burning light source that loses its power when you take damage. Enemies in the darkness also deal substantially higher damage and take less damage. You may slay certain enemies to refresh power of your light source. Should you complete the Dungeon, at the end of it awaits The Vaults of Uncertain Fate that you may spend all your hard earned gold away for great rewards.

Soulfire Bastion is a Dungeon where you fight through undead hordes with two strong damage types, using a Soulfire Shield that switches protection between the two damage types. It is fueled by Souls that you obtain from slaying enemies. These same Souls are then also used to gamble with the Soulfire Gambler at the end of the Dungeon for powerful items.

Temporal Sanctum is a Dungeon where you must travel between two different Eras to get through it, and at the end of it you’ll have the option to use The Eternity Cache to create a Legendary item by combining an Exalted and Unique item with Legenary Potential.

Similarly, there are effects like “Increased Amulet Drop Rate” which are typically granted by Blessings that you can obtain from the Monolith of Fate. This would cause more amulets (including uniques) to drop for you without affecting how many items of other types drop.
Modifiers that increase the quality or quantity of found items like “Increased Item Rarity” or “Increased Amulet Drop Rate” do not compete with modifiers that grant character power.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Havoc:</color> Shuffles the tiers of the non-sealed affixes on an exalted item with 4 non-sealed affixes. For example, an item with tier 2 strength, tier 7 dexterity, tier 3 health and tier 2 endurance might be changed to have tier 7 strength, tier 3 dexterity, tier 2 health, and tier 2 endurance.
Ward Decays more quickly as you gain more of it, but this decay can be slowed with Ward Retention stat.
Ward retention can be found on items and the passive tree, and the Intelligence <link="attributes">attribute</link> also grants Ward Retention.

Defeating Shade in a Timeline that has much lower corruption compared the Timeline with the highest corruption, making it much faster to progress corruption when moving between Timelines. The amount of Bonus Corruption defeating a Shade will be shown on the Echo before entering it.

Defeating Shade with <link="gaze of orobyss">Gaze of Orobyss</link> grants additional Corruption.
If the Corruption on a timeline has been increased, it can be lowered by completing Sanctuary of Eterra Echo connected to the entry point.
Defeating the boss of an Empowered Timeline will grant 1 stack of <link="gaze of orobyss">Gaze of Orobyss.</link>

Gaze of Orobyss is a mechanic that increases the amount of Corruption gained from slaying <link="Corruption and the Shade">Shade of Orobyss</link>, and will become available in Empowered <link="Empowered Timelines">Empowered Timelines.</link>

Gaze of Orobyss is gained by slaying Timeline Bosses, and each stack grants additional 12 Corruption when Shade of Orobyss is slain. You will consume up to 4 Gazes of Orobyss per Shade of Orobyss fight, and remaining Gaze will be saved for subsequent fights.
Dying to Shade of Orobyss while having Gaze of Orobyss will reduce your number of Gaze by 1.
While playing in a party, each party member that was granted an option to choose a Blessing on Timeline completion will also be granted 1 Gaze of Orobyss.

Harbingers are powerful servants of Orobyss, who you will encounter once you have progressed deep in to the <link="Monolith Introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>. Your battle against them is tracked with the <link="Forgotten Knights">Forgotten Knights</link> Faction.
Should you succeed in hunting them down, you’ll eventually be able to challenge their leader.
Endgame Factions are different factions that track the progression of certain game systems. They can be found in the factions panel (bound to Y by default).

Unlike <link="Item Factions">Item Factions</link> where you can only be aligned with one of them per character at once, Endgame Factions are not mutually exclusive. You can belong to an endgame faction and an item faction at the same time, and you can belong to multiple endgame factions at the same time.

Different Endgame Factions may provide different rewards and challenges from others, and their progression isn’t always as simple as just slaying enemies or gaining and spending a specific currency tied to them.

<link="Forgotten Knights">Forgotten Knights</link> is an Endgame Faction that guides you through the progression of defeating the <link="Harbingers">Harbingers</link> that you will encounter in <link="Monolith Introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>.
Forgotten Knights is an <link="Endgame Factions">Endgame Faction</link> that tracks your battle against the powerful servants of Orobyss - <link="Harbingers">The Harbingers</link>.

You will start encountering them once you have progressed deep into the <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>. If you succeed in hunting them all down, you will eventually be able to face their mysterious leader.
Evade is a movement ability that rapidly moves your character forwards (bound to Space by default), and has two charges.
You are not immune to damage and enemy abilities will still be able to hit you while using Evade. Traversal skills do not share cooldown with Evade.

Evade has a cooldown of 4 seconds, and it gains 0.5% Increased Cooldown Recovery Speed per Character Level, lowering the cooldown to 2.7 seconds at Level 100 with no other sources of increased cooldown recovery speed.
Exiled Mages are powerful mages imprisoned and banished through time by the leaders of an order of Runemasters for their crimes.

Free them from their Rune Prisons if you dare, as untold eons of imprisonment may have driven them mad. The knowledge of the Exiled Mages can be found in the form of powerful items they experimented with, though this knowledge shall remain if you do not accept the risk and free them.
Through <link="woven echoes">Woven Echoes</link>, you may be able to learn the secret to crafting your own <link="experimental items">Experimental Items</link>.

Nemeses are formed from the remaining equipment of powerful warriors slain by the <link="Harbingers">Harbingers</link>. Animated by the Harbingers' lingering aura of despair, their animosity towards those that slew their former bearers extends to all travelers, not just those who serve Orobyss as Harbingers. They lie in wait for one to attempt to claim their items, and then coalesce into a formidable being of metal and wrath to exact vengeance.

Free them from their torment and use the trapped magic to empower items as your reward. Some Nemeses in the endgame may even allow you to exchange their unique for one of the player's unique items with no <link="Legendary Items">Legendary Potential</link> or <link="Weaver's Will">Weaver's Will</link>, allowing it to be transformed into a Legendary or to be enhanced with Legendary Potential.

Health is the final defensive layer in Last Epoch and If your health reaches 0, your character dies. Combine with other defensive mechanics such as <link="resistances">resistances</link>, <link="armor">armor</link>, <link="dodge">dodge</link>, <link="health leech">leech</link>, <link="ward">ward</link> and many others to make sure you health never reaches 0.
Health values can be increased through items, passives and by leveling up your character. Each character level grants +8 health.

Your character will also have some passive health regeneration, and it can be increased by skills, items, passives and leveling up your character. Each character level grants +0.125 health regeneration.
Mana is the main resource that is needed to use most skills. As long as your mana is above 0, you are able to use a skill even if the mana cost is higher than your remaining mana.
Mana values can be increased through items, passives and by leveling up your character. Each character level grants 0.5 mana.

When using skills that cost more mana than the amount of mana you currently have, you will go to negative mana. You can still regen mana and use skills that do not have a mana cost while at negative mana, however skills that cost even 1 mana cannot be used while at negative mana. Some skills have benefits while being at negative mana.
Your character will also have 8 mana regeneration by default, which can be increased through skills, items and passives, but not by leveling your character up.

The Map of Eterra (bound to M by default) provides lots of useful information about the world. You can find important story locations, keep track of your campaign progression, use waypoints to travel between different areas and Eras, and see how time and the Void has shaped the world from what it once was.

At the top of the World Map UI, you can see a timeline with four different periods of time, or Eras. Through your travels in Eterra you visit these different Eras many times, and by clicking at any of them you can see the what Eterra looks like in different Eras throughout its history.

On the left side of the World Map, you have a quest log that consists of your current active main and side quests, completed quests and the ability to pin or unpin quests that are then shown on the right side of your UI while you are playing.
Clicking on a quest will bring a more detailed quest information and changes the Era of your UI and show where and when that quest is located at.
Main quests are displayed with golden color, while side quests are in silver.

You can also hover on a specific area or Era that has quest icons to see the names of those quests, and by right-clicking on an area with a unlocked waypoint you can immediately travel there from anywhere.

At the bottom left corner you can see how many of the maximum unlockable passive point and idol slot quest rewards you have obtained from completing quests. There are much more quests providing these rewards than the maximum, so you can choose to complete different quests while being able to get the maximum passive point and idol slot rewards.
Certain enemies have small notches at the bottom section of their health bar, indicating that they are benefitting from the Boss Ward system.

When enough damage is dealt to these enemies and their health bar reaches a notch, it is consumed and the enemy is granted ward based on their maximum health. When a notch is consumed that grants the boss ward, they will also be stunned for a short duration.
Unlike the <link="ward">player ward</link> which decays faster the more ward you have, the rate at which the Boss Ward decays speeds up over time.
Enemies gain 1% penetration per area level, capped at 75% in areas level 75 and above. <link="terminology">Overcapping</link> resistances above 75% does not help mitigating this penetration.

Overcapped Resistance: Any resistance above the cap of 75% is considered overcapped resistance. Overcapped resistance is helpful in mitigating debuffs that lower you resistance, but does not mitigate penetrations.
Plague deals poison damage over time and spreads between enemies. By default it deals 150 damage over 4 seconds. Plague does not stack.
Plague will attempt to spread to a target within 6m every 0.6 seconds. Plague will also attempt to spread if the entity dies before Plague expires.
Enemies that are blinded are unable to deal <link="critical strikes">critical strikes</link>.
Withering causes affected targets to take 10% increased Curse Damage Taken, and lasts 3 secnods. Can stack up to 10 times and has 60% less effect against players and bosses.
Exposed Flesh is a curse which reduces cold resistance by 15%, reduces armor by 15 per point of inflicter’s dexterity, and increases chance to be frozen by 30%.
You can view how many Runes and Glyphs you have in the Crafting window or the Crafting Crafting Materials panel by clicking the button in your inventory.

When interacting with the Nemesis, there are 3 options to choose from:

<#E5E5E5>Banish</color> will remove the Nemesis and avoid a fight. Your next Nemesis encounter will be with a new Nemesis that will have a new set of item rewards.

<#E5E5E5>Challenge</color> will start the fight with the Nemesis and if defeated, will grant its items as rewards. Your next Nemesis encounter will be with a new Nemesis that will have a new set of item rewards.

<#E5E5E5>Empower</color> will start the fight with the Nemesis and if defeated, it will escape through a rift. You will then encounter this same Nemesis later, and it and its items have been Empowered. This upgrades items in various ways, such as gaining affixes, increasing tiers of existing affixes, or adding <link="forging potential">Forging Potential</link>. You can choose to Empower the Nemesis and its items twice before you must either Challenge or Banish it.

Experience is gained by completing quests, slaying enemies and from other rewards, such as <link="monolith introduction">Monolith</link> Echoes. As you gain experience, your character and skills will <link="levels">level up</link>, allowing you to customize and make your character stronger. The maximum character level in Last Epoch is 100. You can still gain experience at level 100, it just won’t increase character level anymore.

A common way to increase the amount of experience gained is by increasing Corruption in Monolith of Fate, but there are other ways to increase experience such as Experience Shrines. Items in Last Epoch do not provide bonuses to experience gained.
Magic enemies provide 50% more experience gained when slain.
Rare enemies provide 200% + additional 4% per level, which means that level 100 rare enemies provide 600% more experience when slain.
Boss enemies do not have an experience gain multiplier, but have a much higher base experience reward when slain than other enemies.

If a lower level player is in a much higher level area, they gain experience from slaying enemies as if the area was up to 10 levels higher than their character. For example, a level 20 character in a level 40 area would gain experience as if the area was level 30.

When playing together with other players and there are big level differences, the previous 10 level difference can go down to a minimum of 2, based on the level difference between the characters. For example, if a level 10 character and a level 70 character were playing together in a level 40 area, the level 10 character would gain experience as if the area was level 12, and the level 70 will gain experience from level 40 area as normal.

Players that are 5 or more levels below the starting level of a chapter will gain less experience from completing quests relative to their character level. For example, completing quests in Chapter 9 will grant much less experience to a character that is level 5, than to a character that is level 50.

Additional bonuses which increase the experience gained in <link="arena">Arena</link> and Monolith Echoes won’t apply to characters under level 50, but they will still gain the unmodified amount of experience from slain enemies.
The amount of <link="terminology">Favor</link> gained while aligned with an <link="item factions">Item Faction</link> is based on the experience you gain.
Damage Taken From Mana Before Health

Damage Taken From Mana Before Health is a defensive mechanic primarily available for Mages, which is mostly obtained through items. Sorcerers, a mastery specialization for Mages, also have this stat available in their passive trees.

When a character has any % of Damage Taken From Mana Before Health, a portion of the damage will be dealt to their mana instead of health. However, if they are at 0 or negative mana, then all damage is applied to health instead. Damage Taken From Mana Before Health stat does not protect ward, similar to <link="endurance">endurance</link>.
Having more than 100% Damage Taken From Mana Before Health does not have any additional effect.

1 Point of mana shields 5 points of health. For example, if a character has 90% of Damage Taken From Mana Before Health and takes 1000 damage after all other reductions to damage taken from other defensive stats, such as <link="armor">armor</link> and <link="resistances">resistances</link> have been applied:
-100 Damage is dealt to health, as 10% of 1000 is 100.
-180 Damage is dealt to mana, as 90% of 1000 is 900, which is then reduced by dividing by 5, which results in 180. This means that effectively your mana has 80% damage reduction.
By default each stack deals 50 damage over 3 seconds and increases the chance to be frozen by 20% (to a maximum of 300% with 15 stacks).

The increase in chance to be frozen is multiplicative with a hit’s chance to freeze.  For example if a hit had 10% chance to freeze by default and the target had 4 stacks of Frostbite the hit would have a 14% chance to freeze instead.

Increased chance to be stunned from shock is multiplicative with your chance to stun an enemy. If you have a 5% chance to stun a Boss before applying any shocks to it, with 10 stacks of shock (80% increased chance to be stunned) your chance to stun the boss would be 9%.
Bosses are harder to stun. In the calculation above, their maximum health is 50% higher. So if a boss has 10 000 health, for the stun calculation 15 000 is used instead.
Bosses are harder to freeze. In the calculation above, their maximum health is 50% higher. So if a boss has 10 000 health, for the freeze calculation 15 000 is used instead.

A skill with 40 freeze rate and 500% freeze rate multiplier would have a 2.4% chance to freeze an enemy that has 10 000 maximum health and 0 current ward:


(40 x 6) / 10 000 = 0.024

If you then apply 3 stacks of chill to that enemy with 50% freeze rate per stack of chill for a total of 150%, your chance to freeze would be 6%:


(40 x 2.5 x 6) / 10 000 = 0.06

If the enemy also has 15 stacks of <link="frostbite">frostbite</link> that add another 300% chance to be frozen, the chance to freeze that enemy would be 24%:


((40 x 2.5 x 6) / 10 000) x 4 = 0.24

If that enemy was a boss, the chance to freeze it would be 16%, as bosses are harder to <link="stun">stun</link> and freeze due to their health being 50% higher in these calculations.


((40 x 2.5 x 6) / (10 000 x 1.50)) x 4 = 0.16
Starting from level 26, all minions gain 0.6% more damage and 0.8% damage reduction per level. At level 100, your minions deal 45% more damage and have 60% damage reduction from this level scaling.
While only Bosses are able to benefit from the Boss Ward mechanic, not all Boss enemies have it.

As you complete the quests given by the Forgotten Knight and defeat stronger Harbingers, your rank within the faction raises. Each rank grants different benefits that include better rewards from <link="Nemesis">Nemeses</link> and bonuses that allow you to climb <link="corruption and the shade">Corruption</link> faster in the Monolith of Fate.

Stash Priority lets you define rules for how certain items should be moved into your stash when quick storing (shift + right click or X / Square on controller). If any tab has a matching priority when quick storing an item, it will be placed in that tab, even if not currently viewed. Manually placing items directly in a slot in a tab bypasses Stash Priority.

<#E5E5E5>Configuring Stash Priority</color>
You can set priority conditions on any tab when configuring it (right click the tab or use the configure tab button while viewing it) by checking the Stash Priority checkbox. To remove priority conditions, uncheck this and save the tab.

Stash Priority supports the following conditions, similar to <link="loot filter">Loot Filters</link>:
Item Type
- Subtype (if only 1 item type is selected)

<link="Rarity">Rarity</link>
- <link="legendary items">Legendary Potential</link> Min/Max (if only Unique is selected)
- <link="weaver's will">Weaver's Will</link> Min/Max (if only Unique is selected)

Class Requirement
- For example, you can set a tab to only acceptitems if they can be equipped by a Priamlist or a Mage.

Level Requirement
- For example, a tab could be set to only accept items if they have a minimum level requrement of 80 to equip, or a maximum level of 70 to equip.

<link="item factions">Item Faction</link> Requirement
- For example, some items drop with a Circle of Fortune requirement, and all items traded between players in Merchant's Guild will require to be part of the Merchant's Guild to equip.
All priority conditions on a tab must match for an item to be automatically placed there.
For example, a tab with priority conditions of Rarity: Exalted and Item Type: Helmet will match exalted helmets, but not exalted boots or unique helmets.

<#E5E5E5>How Priority Chooses Tabs To Send Items To </color>
You can set priority conditions on any tab when configuring it (right click the tab or use the configure tab button while viewing it) by checking the Stash Priority checkbox. To remove priority conditions, uncheck this and save the tab.

When multiple stash tabs have a priority that matches an item, it will be placed in the first tab that matches and has space for the item. This respects your custom ordering and will pick the leftmost tab in the leftmost category that matches.
If there is no stash tab with matching priority, or all tabs with matching priority do not have space to fit the item, it is instead placed in the currently viewed tab if possible.

Shrines are objects that a player can interact with to activate them. There is a wide variety of different effects and buffs that occur when they are activated. For example, they can spawn creatures to aid you in combat, drop items, buff the player or cast skills while the shrine is active.
Loot Lizards are peaceful creatures burdened by the weight of items they carry. You can help unburden them by attacking them. They will flee when attacked.
There are several different types of Loot Lizards that carry different items, and they can be distinguished from each other by their color.
When reduced to zero health, Loot Lizards will escape. If they have not been damaged for 20 seconds, they will also escape. This timer will reset whenever any damage is dealt to them.
Members of The Woven faction are devoted to a mysterious being known as The Weaver. Not much of her is known, other than her goal to weave a new, perfect world.

The Faction can be joined by talking to Masque inside the Haven of Silk after the completion of a <link="terminology">Cemetery of the Erased</link>.  You can further improve your rank by earning and spending <link="terminology">Memory Amber</link>, which is most commonly found inside <link="terminology">Tombs</link> and Cemeteries of the Erased, or by slaying the Weaver's creations.

Echoes containing a Tomb or a Cemetery will always have <link="terminology">Silken Cocoons</link>. These destructible objects are found inside and around the entrances to Tombs and Cemeteries. The cocoons can be smashed open to either release a Soul of the Erased, which will empower a nearby enemy, or instead release an enemy trapped inside.

Ranks within the Weaver Faction provide two things; Points to spend in the <link="weaver tree">Weaver Tree</link>, and the ability to purchase new <link="woven echoes">Woven Echoes</link> from Masque. Spending points in the Weaver Tree allows players to tailor their preferred rewards and content inside the <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>.

Woven Echoes are unfinished glimpses into the new world created by The Weaver. Woven Echoes can be woven in to your <link="timelines">Timeline's</link> Echo Web through the <link="terminology">Altar to The Weaver</link>, which can be found inside <link="terminology">Cemeteries of the Erased</link>. Once woven in to your Echo Web, they can then be entered the same as any other Echo.
Woven Echoes are most often found inside Tombs and Cemeteries of the Erased, but many can also be bought from Masque inside the Haven of Silk by spending <link="terminology">Memory Amber</link>.
There are dozens of unique Woven Echoes and they are stored in their own inventory tab.

Woven Echoes provide a range of new encounters with powerful rewards, such as the ability to attempt increasing the <link="legendary items">Legendary Potential</link> on Unique items which already have at least 1 Legendary Potential.

The Weaver Tree is a passive tree you can use to influence the <link="echoes">Echoes</link> inside the <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>.  Collect points to use in the Weaver Tree by increasing your Rank within the <link="the woven">Woven Faction</link> and by completing <link="woven echoes">Woven Echoes</link>.

Champions are a subset of Rare enemies, with proper names and more powerful abilities. They often have their own modifiers, which do not spawn on other enemies. They can be recognized by their names, minimap icon, and an orange highlight.

Once defeated, Champions always drop an item with a sealed Champion Affix based on the Champion's modifiers. Champion Affixes only occur on items, they cannot be obtained as shards via <link="runes and glyphs">Rune of Shattering</link> or <link="runes and glyphs">Rune of Removal</link>.

These Set Affixes can then be crafted onto normal, magic, rare or exalted items of the same type from which that Set Affix came from, creating a Reforged Set item. This Reforged Set item counts as a Set item for the Set bonuses.

Memory Amber: The currency used within the <link="the woven">Woven Faction</link>. It can be used to purchase <link="woven echoes">Woven Echoes</link> or to respec points inside the <link="weaver tree">Weaver Tree</link>.

Tombs of the Erased: Procedurally generated side zones found inside <link="echoes">Echoes</link>. Completing them usually grants a duplicate Echo reward, and is the primary source for collecting Memory Amber to use within the <link="the woven">Woven Faction</link>.

Cemeteries of the Erased: Procedurally generated side zones similar to Tombs that can be found in the <link="monolith introduction">Monolith of Fate</link>. Once a Cemetery is completed, you can interact with the Altar to the Weaver to place Woven Echoes on to your <link="timelines">Timeline's</link> Echo Web.
The Altar to the Weaver: An object found at the end of Cemeteries of the Erased, which allows you to place Woven Echoes directly to your current Echo Web.

Silken Cocoons: Destructible objects found in Echoes containing a Tomb or Cemetery of the Erased. Once destroyed, they either release a Soul of the Erased to empower a nearby enemy or an enemy called The Incubated Silk. Both are a good source of obtaining Memory Amber.
<#E5E5E5>Rune of Redemption:</color> Randomly changes the exalted affixes on an item into different affixes. Does not change the tiers of the affixes or the rolls within the tiers.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Weaving:</color> Uses a random amount of the remaining <link="weaver's will">Weaver's Will</link> or <link="weaver's touch">Weaver's Touch</link> on an item to add or improve affixes as if you had slain many enemies with the item equipped.

Dungeon Key Charms are modifier items that can be used to modify your Dungeon experience. For example, you can add lots of <link="loot lizards">Loot Lizards</link> or open a shortcut portal directly to fight the boss.
Dungeon Keys can be used to purchase Dungeon Key Charms in Divine Era’s Maj’elka.

Among the ability to influence what type and frequency of content you can encounter, the Weaver Tree also provides many other useful perks. Examples include: the option to target farm items in new ways, allowing Woven Echoes to spawn naturally as new types of Echoes, disabling some types of Echoes from spawning entirely (such as Arena Echoes), and increasing the potency of rewards and difficulty of encounters.

Weaver's Touch is a special mechanic on certain idols representing the total Affix Tiers the idol will gain at maximum when fully revealed by slaying enemies, similar to <link="weaver's will">Weaver's Will.</link>

Weaver's Touch can be applied to idols at the end of <link="terminology">Tombs</link> and <link="terminology">Cemeteries of the Erased</link> for no cost, if they do not yet have Weaver's Touch or additional affixes granted by it. When an idol gains Weaver's Touch, it also gets one revealed affix at the same time. When Weaver's Touch is consumed by slaying enemies, it can either reveal a second affix on the idol, or increase the tiers of the first affix. The maximum amount of total affixes an idol can have is 14, which will result in two tier 7 affixes once fully revealed.
If the resulting affixes or amount of Weaver's Touch on an idol is undesirable, those can be rerolled wiith <link="terminology">Memory Amber</link>.

Weaver's Touch is a special mechanic on class specific idols representing the total amount of Affix Tiers the idol will gain at when fully revealed by slaying enemies, similar to <link="weaver's will">Weaver's Will.</link>

Weaver's Touch can be applied to idols at the end of <link="terminology">Tombs</link> and <link="terminology">Cemeteries of the Erased</link> for no cost, if they do not yet have Weaver's Touch or additional affixes granted by it. When an idol gains Weaver's Touch, it also gets one revealed affix at the same time. Weaver's Touch is consumed by slaying enemies, and it will either reveal a second affix on the idol, or increase the tiers of the first affix. The maximum amount of total affixes an idol can have is 14, which will result in two tier 7 affixes once fully revealed.
If the resulting affixes or amount of Weaver's Touch on an idol is undesirable, those can be rerolled with <link="terminology">Memory Amber</link>.

<#E5E5E5>Rune of Evolution:</color> Evolves an exalted item into a <link="primordial items">Primordial</link> exalted item, upgrading a random tier 7 affix on the item into a tier 8 affix, massively increasing the affix’s power and moving it to its own second sealed affix slot.
Primordial Items are a powerful type of equipment, with a restriction of only being able to wield one at a time.

There are two types of Primordial items, Exalted and Unique. Primordial exalted affixes are tier 8, and are primarily created by using a <link="runes and glyphs">Rune of Evolution</link> on an item with at least one tier 7 affix on it. Primordial uniques are similar to other <link="uniques">Unique items</link> in that they can also be crafted to <link="Legendary Items">Legendary items</link>, but usually come with some more extraordinary effects. Most Primordial Unique items are created by Skarven Bloodhorn by giving him <link="rift beasts">Rift Beasts'</link> body parts.

While aligned with Merchant’s Guild, two different tokens can be found that bypass some trade restrictions:
 - Merchant’s Token allows the purchase of an item at the Bazaar while ignoring the Favor cost.
 - Black Market Token allows the purchase of an item at the Bazaar while ignoring the Favor cost and Rank requirement, and any item purchased with a Black Market Token will only require Rank 1 in Merchant’s Guild to equip.
Rift Beasts are ancient and powerful creatures that possess the ability to tear rifts between eras. This allows them to hunt down their prey with swift ambushes wherever and whenever they please.

Sometimes Rift Beasts leave larger rifts behind that lead to their dens in the Ancient Era, where they lay their eggs and prepare for future hunts. After defeating a Rift Beast in its den, you can choose which of its offspring survives, effectively deciding how future Rift Beasts you encounter will have evolved.

Different evolutions cause the Rift Beasts to gain different abilities and grow different body parts. Some of these body parts can be harvested and exchanged with Skarven Bloodhorn for powerful items such as <link="primordial items">Primordial Unique items</link> and <link="runes and glyphs">Runes of Evolutions</link>.
The stash supports searching for items based on the contents of their tooltip as well as macros, regex, and expressions to compose more specific searches.
When searching in the Quick View sidebar, your tabs will be filtered to only show those that contain at least one matching item.

Usage Examples - T6+ or 1T6+ matches any exalted item (at least 1 affix is tier 6 or higher) - 2T7 matches double T7 exalted items (at least 2 affixes are tier 7) - 3T6+&T7 matches triple exalted items where at least 1 affix is T7 (e.g. T7/T7/T7 or T7/T6/T6 would both match) - WW&PT20+ would match Weaver's Will items that have 20 or more Weaver's Will and legendary affix tiers - LP3+|WW20+ to find high potential uniques - prefixes1&T7 to find T7 exalts with an open prefix - T7&/boots/ to find boots with at least 1 T7 affix - /crit/&/ring/&LP1+ to find LP1+ rings that have crit
Regex - Wrap your search in / to search using case-insensitive regex - e.g. /1[4-9] attunement/ to find items with 14-19 attunement

Macros Macros are case-insensitive short hands that match supported properties of items with an optional quantifier e.g. - LP0 (is unique with no legendary potential) - T6+ (has tier 6 or higher affix) - prefixes2 (has 2 prefixes) Most macros match a number on the item and are of the form: Macro[number][+/-] If a number is provided, the item must exactly match that number. If + or - is provided, the item must be greater/less than or equal to that number. For example: - WW matches any Weaver’s Will item and the macro matches the amount of Weaver’s Will on the item - WW0 matches any Weaver’s Will item with no Weaver’s Will left - WW15+ matches any Weaver’s Will item with at least 15 Weaver’s Will
Expressions Macros and regex can be combined into expressions - Combining with | means either the left or right macro must match - Combining with & means both the left and right macro must match

Supported Macros - Item Potential - LP (is non-WW unique) - WW (is WW unique or legendary) - WT (is enchantable idol) - FP (is forgeable equipment) - PT (potential tier) matches items that could be used for unique rerolling in the Gauntlet of Strife.

Affix Tier Count
- These work the same as affix tier macros, but instead of matching any affix, they match if at least a certain number of affixes are matching tiers
- Available affix tier counts are 1-5 (1T, 2T, 3T, 4T, 5T)
